era_context: Late Archaic Greece; the Peisistratid tyranny continues in Athens. Sparta is the dominant land power in Greece. The Persian Empire controls Asia Minor and the eastern Mediterranean. In Mesopotamia, Nebuchadnezzar II continues his reign, consolidating Babylonian power. In India, the early Vedic period is transitioning toward the Upanishadic age and the rise of heterodox philosophical movements. In China, the Spring and Autumn period continues with competing warring states.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Most of the Mediterranean world (no specific events recorded)

events.title: Second Sacred War in Greece — disputed dating and significance
events.description: A conflict erupts in central Greece over control of the sanctuary of Apollo at Delphi and the Amphictyonic League (a religious and political association of Greek city-states). The Second Sacred War (also called the War of the Phocians) is traditionally dated to c. 595–585 BCE, though the exact chronology is disputed. The conflict involves Phocis, Thessaly, and other Greek states competing for influence over the Delphic sanctuary. The war is attested in later Greek sources (Herodotus, Pausanias) but no contemporary documentation survives. The significance of this conflict in Greek political history is debated; some scholars view it as a minor regional dispute, while others see it as an important moment in the development of Greek interstate relations.
key_figures: Cleisthenes of Sicyon (possibly involved, though dating is uncertain)

sources.name: Herodotus, Histories (5th century BCE, written ~100 years after events)
Pausanias, Description of Greece (2nd century CE, much later compilation)
Strabo, Geography (1st century BCE/CE, compilation of earlier sources)

events.certainty_note: The Second Sacred War is attested in later Greek sources, but the exact dating is uncertain. Herodotus and other ancient sources provide conflicting chronologies. Modern scholars debate whether the war occurred in the 590s or 580s BCE. No contemporary documentation survives. The traditional date of c. 595–585 BCE is based on later historical reconstructions.
cross_references: -585-001 (end of Second Sacred War, alternative dating)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Second Sacred War is highly disputed. Ancient sources (Herodotus, Pausanias) provide conflicting accounts, and modern scholars have proposed dates ranging from 600 to 580 BCE. Some scholars question whether the war occurred at all or whether it is a conflation of multiple conflicts. The significance of the war in Greek history is also debated; it may have been a minor regional dispute rather than a major political event. The absence of contemporary documentation makes it impossible to verify the details provided by later sources.
historiographic_note: This year is poorly documented in surviving sources. The Second Sacred War is the only event that can be tentatively assigned to this period, and even that assignment is uncertain. The war is known only through later Greek historians writing centuries after the events, and their accounts are inconsistent. No contemporary documentation (inscriptions, coins, archaeological evidence) has been found to corroborate the details. The historical record for this year is heavily dependent on the reliability of later Greek historiography, which is known to contain errors, anachronisms, and legendary material. For most of the world outside the Mediterranean and Near East, no written records survive for this year.

graph_edges.note: The Second Sacred War is traditionally dated to c. 595–585 BCE; the exact duration and endpoint are uncertain
